mnemonics.title: MOV Quick Check
mnemonics.content: To check if constant fits in MOV: Write in hex, check if two matching 8-bit halves exist that can be rotated. Example: 0xFF0000FF works (rotate 0xFF by 24) but 0x12345678 doesn't.

mnemonics.title: Literal Pool Placement
mnemonics.content: Pools must be KEPT CLOSE (within 4KB) to code. Use LTORG to place pools explicitly or assembler auto-places them after functions.

acronyms.usage: Loads bitwise complement of value into register. Useful for constants like 0xFFFF (MVN #0)
Loads PC-relative address into register, faster than memory load
Pseudo-instruction for ±64KB address range, expands to 2 instructions
Loads 32-bit word from memory; LDR =const is pseudo-instruction for loading constants
Directive to force assembler to place literal pool at current location
Register pointing to next instruction; used for PC-relative addressing in literal pools

visualTips.visualization: Think of 32-bit MOV encoding as: [Rotate(4 bits)][Zeros(4 bits)][Imm8 bits] - rotate 8-bit value by even number to get 32-bit constant
Draw PC pointing to instruction, add 4KB window - anything in that window can be accessed with single LDR PC-relative instruction
Think of register as two 16-bit halves: MOVW fills lower half (bits 0-15), MOVT fills upper half (bits 16-31)

analogies.analogy: MOV is like taking cash from wallet (instant), LDR is like going to ATM (needs memory trip). Use MOV when possible for speed.
Literal pool is like a cheat sheet placed near your desk - you can quickly reference it if it's within arm's reach (4KB), otherwise you need to get up.
MVN is like flipping a photo - all black becomes white and vice versa. 0 becomes 0xFFFFFFFF (all 1s).
